judge.validator_python:
def validate(args, output):
    preorder, postorder = args[0], args[1]
    def deser(a):
        if not a:
            return None
        nodes = [[a[0], None, None]]
        idx = 1
        i = 0
        while i < len(nodes) and idx < len(a):
            node = nodes[i]
            if idx < len(a):
                v = a[idx]; idx += 1
                if v is not None:
                    node[1] = len(nodes)
                    nodes.append([v, None, None])
            if idx < len(a):
                v = a[idx]; idx += 1
                if v is not None:
                    node[2] = len(nodes)
                    nodes.append([v, None, None])
            i += 1
        return nodes
    nodes = deser(output)
    if nodes is None:
        return len(preorder) == 0
    def pre(i, acc):
        if i is None:
            return
        val, l, r = nodes[i]
        acc.append(val)
        pre(l, acc)
        pre(r, acc)
    def post(i, acc):
        if i is None:
            return
        val, l, r = nodes[i]
        post(l, acc)
        post(r, acc)
        acc.append(val)
    pre_acc = []
    pre(0, pre_acc)
    post_acc = []
    post(0, post_acc)
    return pre_acc == preorder and post_acc == postorder

pattern: The second value in a preorder listing is always the root of the left branch, and its position in the postorder listing marks exactly where that whole left branch ends, letting the two arrays be split recursively.
hints: The first entry of preorder and the last entry of postorder are both the current root; that much is always unambiguous.
If more than one node remains, the second preorder entry is the root of the left branch; locate that same value's position in postorder to learn how many nodes belong to the left branch.

Split both arrays at that boundary and recurse on the left slice and the right slice separately; a node with only one child can legally be attached on either side, which is why more than one valid tree can exist.

edge_inputs.description: seven distinct nodes, every internal node has two children
two nodes, root with exactly one child, the ambiguous single-child case
a chain of three nodes where every node has exactly one child
root has a single left-heavy branch feeding into a two-child node
thirty distinct nodes forming a perfect binary tree, the maximum size
root's left branch is a single leaf, right branch has two children

stress.description: randomly shaped binary tree of unique values, its true preorder and postorder fed back in
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(1, max(1, min(size, 30)))
    vals = rng.sample(range(1, 31), n)
    nodes = {0: [vals[0], None, None]}
    open_slots = [(0, 'left'), (0, 'right')]
    next_id = 1
    for v in vals[1:]:
        idx = rng.randrange(len(open_slots))
        parent_id, side = open_slots.pop(idx)
        nid = next_id
        next_id += 1
        nodes[nid] = [v, None, None]
        if side == 'left':
            nodes[parent_id][1] = nid
        else:
            nodes[parent_id][2] = nid
        open_slots.append((nid, 'left'))
        open_slots.append((nid, 'right'))
    def pre(i, acc):
        if i is None:
            return
        val, l, r = nodes[i]
        acc.append(val)
        pre(l, acc)
        pre(r, acc)
    def post(i, acc):
        if i is None:
            return
        val, l, r = nodes[i]
        post(l, acc)
        post(r, acc)
        acc.append(val)
    preacc = []
    pre(0, preacc)
    postacc = []
    post(0, postacc)
    return [preacc, postacc]
stress.note: generated preorder/postorder always come from a genuine tree, so a valid reconstruction always exists

complexity.time: O(n^2) worst case (O(n) with a hash index)
